questions.question: Magnitude-based pruning removes:
options: Weights with small absolute values — assumed to contribute least
questions.explanation: Pruning: set weights below a threshold to zero. Structured pruning removes entire neurons/filters for actual speedup.

questions.question: INT8 quantisation reduces memory by approximately:
questions.explanation: float32 = 4 bytes per weight. int8 = 1 byte. 4x compression with ~1% accuracy loss typically.

questions.question: Knowledge distillation trains a student to:
options: Mimic the teacher's output probability distribution (soft labels), not just class labels
Have fewer layers than the teacher
questions.explanation: Soft labels from the teacher reveal inter-class similarities — richer supervision than one-hot labels.

questions.question: Post-training quantisation (PTQ) vs Quantisation-Aware Training (QAT):
options: PTQ quantises after training (fast, small accuracy loss); QAT simulates quantisation during training (slower, more accurate)
questions.explanation: PTQ is easier but less accurate. QAT lets the model adapt to quantisation noise — better for tight accuracy requirements.

questions.question: MobileNet uses depthwise separable convolutions to:
options: Reduce computation by factoring standard convolutions into depthwise + pointwise — ~8-9x fewer FLOPs
questions.explanation: Depthwise: one filter per input channel. Pointwise: 1x1 conv to mix channels. Together ~8x cheaper than standard conv.

questions.question: Neural Architecture Search (NAS) automatically finds:
options: Efficient model architectures for a given compute/accuracy constraint
questions.explanation: NAS searches over architecture space (layer types, connections, widths) to optimise accuracy under hardware constraints.

questions.question: The lottery ticket hypothesis states:
options: Sparse subnetworks exist in dense networks that can achieve comparable accuracy when trained from their original initialisation
Small models can never match large ones
questions.explanation: Frankle & Carlin (2019): a dense network contains a 'winning ticket' sparse subnetwork — identifiable by iterative magnitude pruning.

questions.question: Structured pruning vs unstructured pruning:
options: Structured removes entire filters/channels (actual hardware speedup); unstructured sets individual weights to zero (sparse matrices)
questions.explanation: Sparse matrices from unstructured pruning need special hardware/libraries to accelerate. Structured pruning gives real speedup on standard hardware.

questions.question: Low-rank factorisation compresses a weight matrix W (m×n) by:
options: Decomposing W ≈ U·V where U is (m×r) and V is (r×n) with r << min(m,n)
questions.explanation: Rank-r approximation reduces parameters from m*n to r*(m+n). LoRA uses this to efficiently fine-tune large models.

questions.question: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is used for:
options: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large models by adding small trainable rank-decomposition matrices
questions.explanation: LoRA: freeze pre-trained weights W, add trainable delta_W=B*A (rank r). Only B,A train — <1% of original parameters.
